speaker: interviewer
text: This is the camera. Tape three. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Lidia Eichenholz. November 2, 1995. Minneapolis, Minnesota. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Did I understand you to say that you had someone hidden in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was still under the Russians when, as I mentioned before, Boris Rojzen was a close friend and neighbor of ours. He was a very prominent man in town. He was Sorbonne educated international lawyer. He also served in the army for a long time. After he married, he did not-- he did not do much of his law. He was very busy with his Zionist work. He was a close protegee of Vladimir Jabotinsky-- of Jabotinsky, that-- that was-- which is now a recruit party, but at this time it was party to the right, which considered that, like, that Jews have-- have to fight for their country. They-- they did not believe that it could be achieved just by negotiation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in Dubno?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rowne that was in Rowne.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and he lived close to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was just when the Russians had come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after a while the Russians have come. He was always a very charming man, and I remember he was very close to us kids, because he-- they did not had, in the beginning, the children, so he always visited. And he invented many games and taught us many things. A rather unusual man. If he-- He was actually scheduled to have Menachem Begin's job because of his qualities, which Begin did not have. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when the Russians came they were after him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first they were-- they were always opposed to Zionism. They felt that Russia is a country for all the nationalities, including Jews. Many Jews were prominent in Russia. And anyway, the Russians were very much under people who-- who were leaders or had the potential for leadership. So they were after him, and somehow he found out and he did not sleep at home any more because end of the day, used to come always at night. And he stayed with us for a while. We-- actually, it was not a very good place to hide because we were [INAUDIBLE] by ourself. Nevertheless, one day when they finally-- somebody must have squealed on him. When we finally found-- when they finally came to our place he-- he managed to evade them. He jumped through the window, and then changed his clothes and-- and disappeared. And as I say, that was when the Russians-- when we still lived in Rowne. And only later, in Dubno, I-- I heard that he's a police chief.

speaker: interviewer
text: In--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lwow. Was he involved in your moving to Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was one of the things. And there were other people from Rowne who live in Lwow. And somehow my mother felt-- since my father was dead, and all other men-- that we'd be-- that we will be safer in Lwow, which proved to be a illusion. Lwow was just as-- and Lwow was part of Galicia. That's how-- it was actually-- that's an old name for the Austria-Hungarian monarchy province. Before the World War I, Galicia was part of Austriohungarian monarchy. Poland was dismembered for several centuries, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and many people did speak German in Lwow and Krakow. And-- we-- we-- I don't know why my mother had about this illusion that we will be safer in Lwow, which proved to be really just a hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you move immediately into a ghetto in-- in Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we-- we lived outside of ghetto for a while, and then we move into ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to move, though my mother tried to get some papers. But it was hard because my-- my younger brother-- I-- I-- I look-- I look very inconspicuous, but my mother and my younger brother, they would attract attention. My mother because of her height and dark hair, and of course my brother was circumcised. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were-- what was life like in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was very endangered, just as much as-- we had to go to walk every day, march on the street to do various cleanings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your work was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly-- I did mostly cleaning. My mother worked occasionally in the kitchen, in army kitchen, which was considered to be safer than just cleaning houses of various German officers.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your work, did you-- you worked for the Germans particularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, mostly, all the officers. And my brother had to be left alone in ghetto, which was, again, another source of danger. We knew other people from Rowne in Lwow, and they were also in similar situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Jewish community within the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto had, of course, had all the structures. There was a Jewish community, and all kind-- they tried to organize various workshops, things like that, just to prove that you are useful to-- to Germans. But the ghetto was actually on Zamarstynow, that part of-- it's a poor part of the country. Z-A-M-A-R-S-T-Y-N-O-W. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the ghetto, did you feel frightened? I mean, were people being taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, people were taken. The ghetto was decreasing in size all the time, but so long we had those jobs it seemed that we-- we might be safe. We changed the jobs several times.

speaker: interviewer
text: By changing, you mean you did different work or with different people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, both way. It seems that, you know, if you work for the military or something in the kitchen, then-- then you-- you might be safer than if you worked for some private German people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you relate with-- to these Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was too young. They were not particularly interested in me. And actually, there was a big difference between the civilian Germans and Gestapo people. So and they behaved differently on different occasion. I did not get really to-- to work for this high [INAUDIBLE]. Like, Poland was divided, actually. Like, part of Poland was annexed to Germany, like Lodz and-- and [? Parmadanya. ?] They were the old-- actually, when the old German profession, before the World War I, stayed. So Maybe Lwow-- Lwow ghetto, maybe, had less people. Of course, they collected people from the provincial cities and pulled them all in one place to make it easier for them So there were many people in ghetto who did not have means to buy food or anything else. But we were still lucky with our possession. And that's how it-- we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you think of some examples of how you were treated in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto? Well, once you went to work so you were gone during the day. And, of course, at night everybody was equally frightened. And we had-- as I mentioned before, we had to leave my brother in the ghetto, and that was dangerous. Although he managed, somehow, to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the-- in the ghetto, what was special about the nights? What was frightening about the nights?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people got together and they would tell-- and somehow the-- actually there was no mail, no services, but the news would come. Actually, all the killing took place during the daytime, but people would get-- talk about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the killing pre-determined? Was it random?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at this point I think it was pre-determined, but also several killings at random. There was some stealing going on, and they hung several policemen just at random to--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the Jewish policemen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I don't think they had anything much to do with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just as examples.

speaker: interviewee
text: Example, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon? Yes, there was a Judenrat, and the police-- and-- well, they were the main forces. The Judenrat would give people those cards that they have-- that they have a job, so they should be excluded, or they have a profession which might be useful to Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: In many cases it did, but sometimes you had to-- they saw-- there were also Ukrainian police-- policemen guarding the ghetto. Some were Lithuanian brought to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] taken from the ghettos by train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Mostly by-- when the main killing started, mostly by trucks, actually, open trucks. You could see them. They all had to sit, and they-- they were taken to the-- that would be the eastern side of the city, and-- and the same thing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They were not taken to concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody. Because you see, Galicia and Lwow was-- it was considered to be an eastern province. So they did not bother-- I mean, Janowskastrasse eventually became a small concentration camp, but not others. There were no other concentration camps in eastern Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you leave? I understand you did. How did you leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother paid a lot of money to have me taken out, and the event was that people-- that was actually started with the Ukrainian policemen and some other people. They would-- they would take me out of Lwow, and then bring back to Lwow when-- when the-- when the shooting started, just to-- because in a small town, it was much harder to survive. You were much more conspicuous. And the agreement was that my-- that my mother and my brother would follow me but, of course, they never-- it got too late, all the people who did it for me got frightened to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left the ghetto, you say you left during the--

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit before, a little bit before the shooting.

speaker: interviewer
text: The mass killings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, OK, wait-- there was a camp in between Janowskastrasse. Actually, I left ghetto a couple of times. First, I-- I got-- when ghetto was still there and the camp was not yet in reality, I always felt that the only way to survive is to leave the ghetto, and I tried the very first time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the people who were supposed to give me a shelter, they got frightened. So I had to go back to ghetto. Again, it was arranged by my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult to get out and then back into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it depends. We had to pay for all this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you paying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, mostly-- mostly Ukrainian police, and sometimes not directly. There were contacts in between.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then the camp was not guarded so much by the Germans, but by the Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- I am still talking about ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the ghetto, yeah,

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, both, because German didn't wanted to spell so many people doing such jobs. So there was a-- a Ukrainian police organized.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in a concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, when I got back to ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I think we're missing something. Let's-- when you left the ghetto, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he took me to someplace outside of of-- of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: The man who guided me. But-- but then he came back. I had to come back to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I could not survive. They were frightened to keep me, and they say they will go to police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you're back in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am back in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, then the ghetto was actually liquidated. Only a small amount of people went to Janowskastrasse camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did, my mother, and my brother. Again, this man, Rojzen, was helping us. And-- and Janowskastrasse camp was located in a different part of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't go very far.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at this time it seemed like a big distance, because you were not supposed to leave neither ghetto nor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you went into the camp, were the Germans taking you into the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were Germans in the camp, but also-- also Ukrainian. Because again, they could not-- the commandant was German, by name Katz, K-A-T-Z. And-- but he had many helpers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you go into the camp voluntarily?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had no choice because--

speaker: interviewer
text: The liquidation of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in other words, you were escaping from the liquidation and went into the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Camp-- camp was not-- was not big, so most of the people were already killed before the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of camp was it? Was it a--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a work camp or a killing camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a concentration camp because-- Janowskastrasse was a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Janowskastrasse, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but it was not meant to last long. That's why none of us got tattoo.

speaker: interviewer
text: No numbers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because the whole eastern Poland, really, did not have-- it was not meant, like, to be the area for the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get admitted into the camp, as it were? How did they get your numbers and how were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was all my mother-- did it with-- with people who were high up. They-- they took certain amount of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your life like? What was-- what were you doing in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you had to do it some kitchen work and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Teaching?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kitchen-- cleaning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cleaning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cleaning, and it was very-- it was very hopeless, because we knew that it would not last.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew what would happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We expected that something would happen any minute. The camp was located where the old-- on Janowskastrasse Street. In Poland, prisons or camps are named after the street that they are located. Actually, same is in Poland in Warsaw, so people know. They don't have special names.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the camp, can you tell us what a day was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you had to get up early and there was a lot of fright, especially for us, because of my brother, Joseph. And he was still young, although his age was given older than he was. And then we were taken to-- to cleaning in the camp, or sometimes outside of-- of the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your whole family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, a whole group of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: A whole group of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a lot of people who were very useful. Like, you know, like, they worked in the city, like watchmakers, or something that the Germans needed for--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother and your brother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they did-- they did also, the same kind of work, but then it became clear that we-- that we have to try to get out somehow, because the camp would not last.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to work early in the morning, did you work all day? Did you get a lunch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think we had to bring some bread and water-- water was given.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you go back to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother always managed to-- to secure a little food, but many people did not have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- you mother was a very strong influence at this point, and she-- she did help.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was this small family-- your mother, your brother, and yourself-- how did this work? How did you get-- did you stay in the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's-- it's-- well, we change a little, but she managed to get always better condition for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well he worked, and-- or-- or sometimes he was just hiding because he was too young, clearly, and we were frightened about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the camp was being run by the Germans and the Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Ukrainians. They had to take orders from Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they march you to your work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sometimes. We had to walk on a-- not on a-- on-- not on a sidewalk, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the streets.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of Lwow. And at the end of the day, which was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can-- I think, I don't remember a whole day, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And you went back to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to the camp, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had food at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I say again, my mother always-- I think my brother sometimes did all the cooking and buying bread. Bread was the main thing. You had to pay fortune.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well that was-- that was till-- it lasted approximately till fall, December, '43. But then not-- yes, but then it-- it became clear that it's going-- that we're not going to make-- that the camp won't last much longer. So my mother start making, again, effort that somebody takes us out. It was very hard to find people who will be willing to do such things because there was a death penalty for both those who were Jews and those who were hiding the Jews, so it was very dangerous for the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would happen to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were-- some of them were shot same way as-- they are now being honored in Israel as righteous-- as righteous Gentiles. But it did not happen-- but still they lost their life, or sometimes they were put in prison and treated very badly. So people were actually frightened. We did not know that many people in Lwow by then, because in meantime, we lived in Rowne. So the contacts were lost, and many Polish people left or start leaving Lwow, moving farther to Krakow, or Warsaw, which was considered to be a safer place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these just Polish people, or were they Polish Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these just-- were these Polish Christians or Polish Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, they were-- no, Jewish people could not move at all. They were just-- I mean, once-- a couple of times I tried to-- to leave and make a contact. I-- I looked very blonde and blue eye. Nobody-- and my Polish was very good, so nobody expected anything, that I might be Jewish. Nevertheless, it was very hard to make contact with non-Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some people-- well, it did not happen to me because I survived, but some people, well, they would just pretend they don't know you. Because I think they-- they did not want it, or they-- for various reasons.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was going on when they pretended they didn't know you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they just-- if you approached them, they-- they just go away, and you could not do it second time because that might be considered molesting them, and there was so much police in every--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was your effort to reach out into the Polish Christian community?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was unsuccessful.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, it was unsuccessful. Finally-- finally, we found people who-- who were willing to-- to put me up for a couple days until-- and they were not-- I don't know how much they knew, but they were not afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this for your mother, and your brother, and yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was original agreement, but they never got out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get out of the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, somebody, again, took me out when I was marching to work, and left me with a family outside of the city. And then I had to make my way back to the city. By then, the whole camp was gone. And there were-- there were a number of people involved. And eventually, I stayed with total strangers, and I just start looking for work. German families were coming a lot to-- to Lwow, and they were looking for maids or things like that, and I-- and since I looked like I said before, I found job with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you left the camp and someone took you, this was in the morning when you were going to work. And instead of going to your workplace--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he took me to a place outside of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to a-- a-- a-- a Polish Christian family's home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and they-- and-- and they kept me only for couple days. They were, again, frightened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you couldn't go back to the camp at this point.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, the camp was already been annihilated, just like ghetto before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't very much time between when you left the camp and when the camp was destroyed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everyone in it was killed. And your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did not know all of the details till much later. And then I-- then I-- then I found various cleaning jobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is just out of Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I went back to Lwow because in the small town it was much harder. And I found various cleaning jobs, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which kept me alive. My mother was supposed to bring more money and whatever we had, but that never happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you live? I mean, where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, whatever-- I had to eat whatever they gave it to me, and then-- then I eventually found place in a small basement. You could not even call it a boarding place, but we may call it like that. There were several other women living. I just had one bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had one bed within a room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Within a room, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: With others?

speaker: interviewee
text: With others. We shared--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were about four of us. Each girl had--

speaker: interviewer
text: In a-- in a large room? Small room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Medium size. Size, yeah. And there was a small kitchen in front.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you share food?

speaker: interviewee
text: That whatever little food we have. The other people were non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they did not know who I was, and so long I worked for the Germans. And I had to have police registrations. This is a requirement in Poland. Each time you move from place to place you have to be registered by police, which was a very tricky thing because many people were caught like that. It was a very dangerous place, this employment-- you had to-- meaning you had to have a stamp from employment office.
